For a compact overview of my academic background, see my Curriculum Vitae.

title: First Variation of Area
text: The first variation formula measures how the area of an immersed hypersurface changes under an infinitesimal deformation. Its Euler-Lagrange equation is the minimal-surface equation \(H=0\).

title: First Variation of Area
text:
Geometric Setup
Let \(F_t:\Sigma\to M\) be a smooth one-parameter variation of an immersion \(F=F_0\). Its variational vector field is
\[
X=\left.\frac{\partial F_t}{\partial t}\right|_{t=0}.
\]
Write \(g_t=F_t^*\bar g\) for the induced metric and \(d\mu_t\) for its volume form. Along \(\Sigma\), decompose
\[
X=X^\top+f\nu,
\]
where \(X^\top\) is tangential, \(\nu\) is a unit normal, and \(f=\langle X,\nu\rangle\) is the normal speed.

title: First Variation of Area
text:
Variation of the Metric
For tangent vectors \(e_i,e_j\) on \(\Sigma\),
\[
\left.\frac{d}{dt}\right|_{t=0}(g_t)_{ij}
=\langle \bar\nabla_{e_i}X,e_j\rangle
+\langle e_i,\bar\nabla_{e_j}X\rangle.
\]
Taking the trace and using \(\frac{d}{dt}d\mu_t=\frac12\operatorname{tr}_{g_t}(\dot g_t)d\mu_t\) gives
\[
\left.\frac{d}{dt}\right|_{t=0}d\mu_t
=\bigl(\operatorname{div}_\Sigma X^\top-Hf\bigr)d\mu.
\]
Here the sign convention is \(H=\operatorname{div}_\Sigma\nu\).

title: First Variation of Area
text:
First Variation Formula
Integrating the variation of the area form yields
\[
\left.\frac{d}{dt}\right|_{t=0}\operatorname{Area}(F_t(\Sigma))
=\int_\Sigma \operatorname{div}_\Sigma X^\top\,d\mu
-\int_\Sigma Hf\,d\mu.
\]
If \(\Sigma\) is closed, or if the variation is compactly supported away from \(\partial\Sigma\), the divergence term vanishes. Therefore

\[
\boxed{
\left.\frac{d}{dt}\right|_{t=0}\operatorname{Area}(F_t(\Sigma))
=-\int_\Sigma Hf\,d\mu
}
\]

title: First Variation of Area
text:
Minimality
A hypersurface is stationary for every compactly supported normal variation precisely when
\[
H=0.
\]
Thus minimal hypersurfaces are exactly the critical points of the area functional. This is the geometric analogue of the Euler-Lagrange equation in the calculus of variations.






NoteSign convention



Some authors define the mean curvature with the opposite sign. Under that convention the right-hand side of the first variation formula changes sign, but the stationarity condition remains \(H=0\).

title: Xingjian Zhou
text:
I am a Ph.D. student in mathematics at Xiamen University and a joint Ph.D. student at the University of Trento. My research lies in geometric analysis, especially geometric inequalities and monotonicity formulas arising from elliptic partial differential equations.
This website collects my research, publications, curriculum vitae, and mathematical notes.

Read my notes View publications

Research
My current work focuses on scalar curvature problems, rigidity phenomena, and analytic aspects of the Penrose inequality. See the dedicated Publications page for my peer-reviewed articles and current preprints.

title: Curriculum Vitae
text:
Research Interests
My research lies in geometric analysis, focusing on monotonicity formulas and geometric inequalities associated with elliptic equations arising in differential geometry. Current interests include scalar curvature problems, rigidity phenomena, and analytic aspects of the Penrose inequality.
Keywords: Potential theory; nonlinear elliptic and parabolic PDEs; inverse mean curvature flow; minimal surfaces; scalar curvature.

title: Conformal Transformation Laws
text:
\(\newcommand{\owedge}{\mathbin{\rlap{\bigcirc}\mkern5mu\lower0.02ex{\scriptstyle\wedge}\mkern3mu}}\)
Open PDF version

title: Conformal Transformation Laws
text:
Introduction
We study the transformation laws for geometric quantities under a conformal change of metric. Let \((M^n,g)\) be an \(n\)-dimensional Riemannian manifold, and consider the conformally related metric
\[
\widetilde{g}=e^{2f}g,
\]
where \(f\in C^\infty(M)\).
The Riemann curvature tensor, Ricci curvature tensor, and scalar curvature associated with \(g\) will be denoted by \(\mathrm{Rm}\), \(\mathrm{Ric}\), and \(\mathrm{Sc}\), respectively. Their counterparts with respect to \(\widetilde{g}\) will be denoted by \(\widetilde{\mathrm{Rm}}\), \(\widetilde{\mathrm{Ric}}\), and \(\widetilde{\mathrm{Sc}}\).
Now let \(\Sigma^{n-1}\subset M^n\) be an immersed hypersurface. With respect to \(g\), denote its unit normal, second fundamental form, and mean curvature by \(\nu\), \(A\), and \(H\), respectively. The corresponding quantities computed with respect to \(\widetilde{g}\) will be denoted by \(\widetilde{\nu}\), \(\widetilde{A}\), and \(\widetilde{H}\).
Our goal is to derive the transformation laws for these intrinsic and extrinsic geometric quantities under the conformal change
\[
g\longmapsto \widetilde{g}=e^{2f}g.
\]

title: Conformal Transformation Laws
text:
Geometric Quantities and Conventions
In this section, we fix the notation and sign conventions used throughout the note.

Levi-Civita Connection
Let \(\nabla\) and \(\widetilde{\nabla}\) denote the Levi-Civita connections of \(g\) and \(\widetilde{g}\), respectively. The gradient, Hessian, and Laplace–Beltrami operator associated with \(g\) are defined by
\[
g(\nabla f,X)=df(X),
\]
\[
\nabla^2f(X,Y)=X(Yf)-(\nabla_XY)f,
\]
and
\[
\Delta f=\operatorname{tr}_g\nabla^2f.
\]


Curvature Tensors
We define the Riemann curvature operator by
\[
\mathrm{Rm}(X,Y)Z
=\nabla_X\nabla_YZ
-\nabla_Y\nabla_XZ
-\nabla_{[X,Y]}Z.
\]
Its \((0,4)\)-tensor version is
\[
\mathrm{Rm}(X,Y,Z,W)
=g\bigl(\mathrm{Rm}(X,Y)Z,W\bigr).
\]
The Ricci and scalar curvatures are obtained by taking traces:
\[
\mathrm{Ric}_{jl}=g^{ik}\mathrm{Rm}_{ijkl},
\qquad
\mathrm{Sc}=g^{jl}\mathrm{Ric}_{jl}.
\]
For symmetric \((0,2)\)-tensors \(P\) and \(Q\), we use the Kulkarni–Nomizu product
\[
\begin{aligned}
(P\owedge Q)(X,Y,Z,W)
={}&P(X,Z)Q(Y,W)+P(Y,W)Q(X,Z)\\
&-P(X,W)Q(Y,Z)-P(Y,Z)Q(X,W).
\end{aligned}
\]


Hypersurface Geometry
Let \(\nu\) be a choice of unit normal along \(\Sigma\). For tangent vector fields \(X,Y\in T\Sigma\), we define the second fundamental form by
\[
A(X,Y)=g(\nabla_X\nu,Y).
\]
The mean curvature is the trace of \(A\) with respect to the induced metric:
\[
H=\operatorname{tr}_{g|_{T\Sigma}}A.
\]






NoteSign conventions



The signs in the formulas depend on the conventions for \(\mathrm{Rm}\), \(A\), and \(\Delta\). All formulas below use the conventions fixed above. In particular, \(H\) is the trace of \(A\), rather than its average.

title: Conformal Transformation Laws
text:
Conformal Transformation Formulas
Define the symmetric \((0,2)\)-tensor
\[
B=\nabla^2f-df\otimes df+\frac12|\nabla f|^2g.
\]
With the conventions above, the conformal transformation laws are as follows.

Levi-Civita Connection
For vector fields \(X,Y\) on \(M\),
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\nabla}_XY
=\nabla_XY+X(f)Y+Y(f)X-g(X,Y)\nabla f
}.
\]


Riemann Curvature Tensor
As a \((0,4)\)-tensor,
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\mathrm{Rm}}
=e^{2f}\bigl(\mathrm{Rm}-g\owedge B\bigr)
}.
\]


Ricci Curvature
The Ricci curvature transforms according to
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\mathrm{Ric}}
=\mathrm{Ric}
-(n-2)\bigl(\nabla^2f-df\otimes df\bigr)
-\bigl(\Delta f+(n-2)|\nabla f|^2\bigr)g
}.
\]


Scalar Curvature
The scalar curvature satisfies
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{\mathrm{Sc}}
=e^{-2f}
\left[
\mathrm{Sc}-2(n-1)\Delta f
-(n-1)(n-2)|\nabla f|^2
\right]
}.
\]


Hypersurface Quantities
The unit normal, second fundamental form, and mean curvature transform as
\[
\boxed{\widetilde{\nu}=e^{-f}\nu},
\]
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{A}(X,Y)
=e^f\bigl(A(X,Y)+\nu(f)g(X,Y)\bigr)
},
\]
and
\[
\boxed{
\widetilde{H}
=e^{-f}\bigl(H+(n-1)\nu(f)\bigr)
}.
\]

title: Conformal Transformation Laws
text:
Derivations

Difference of the Levi-Civita Connections
Set
\[
D(X,Y)=\widetilde{\nabla}_XY-\nabla_XY.
\]
Since both connections are torsion-free, \(D\) is symmetric in \(X\) and \(Y\). Applying the Koszul formula to \(\widetilde{g}=e^{2f}g\) gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
2\widetilde{g}(\widetilde{\nabla}_XY,Z)
={}&X\widetilde{g}(Y,Z)+Y\widetilde{g}(X,Z)-Z\widetilde{g}(X,Y)\\
&+\widetilde{g}([X,Y],Z)-\widetilde{g}([X,Z],Y)-\widetilde{g}([Y,Z],X).
\end{aligned}
\]
Using
\[
X\bigl(\widetilde{g}(Y,Z)\bigr)
=e^{2f}\bigl(2X(f)g(Y,Z)+X(g(Y,Z))\bigr)
\]
and comparing with the Koszul formula for \(g\), we obtain
\[
g(D(X,Y),Z)
=X(f)g(Y,Z)+Y(f)g(X,Z)-Z(f)g(X,Y).
\]
Because \(Z(f)=g(\nabla f,Z)\), this yields
\[
D(X,Y)=X(f)Y+Y(f)X-g(X,Y)\nabla f.
\]
Therefore,
\[
\widetilde{\nabla}_XY
=\nabla_XY+X(f)Y+Y(f)X-g(X,Y)\nabla f.
\]


Transformation of the Riemann Curvature Tensor
For two torsion-free connections related by \(\widetilde{\nabla}=\nabla+D\), their curvature operators satisfy
\[
\begin{aligned}
\widetilde{\mathrm{Rm}}(X,Y)Z
={}&\mathrm{Rm}(X,Y)Z
+(\nabla_XD)(Y,Z)-(\nabla_YD)(X,Z)\\
&+D(X,D(Y,Z))-D(Y,D(X,Z)).
\end{aligned}
\]
Substituting
\[
D(X,Y)=X(f)Y+Y(f)X-g(X,Y)\nabla f
\]
and collecting the Hessian and gradient terms gives
\[
e^{-2f}\widetilde{\mathrm{Rm}}
=\mathrm{Rm}-g\owedge
\left(
\nabla^2f-df\otimes df+\frac12|\nabla f|^2g
\right).
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\widetilde{\mathrm{Rm}}
=e^{2f}\bigl(\mathrm{Rm}-g\owedge B\bigr).
\]


Transformation of the Ricci Curvature
Contracting the first and third indices of \(g\owedge B\) gives
\[
g^{ik}(g\owedge B)_{ijkl}
=(n-2)B_{jl}+(\operatorname{tr}_gB)g_{jl}.
\]
Moreover,
\[
\operatorname{tr}_gB
=\Delta f+\frac{n-2}{2}|\nabla f|^2.
\]
Since \(\widetilde{g}^{ik}=e^{-2f}g^{ik}\), tracing the transformed Riemann tensor yields
\[
\begin{aligned}
\widetilde{\mathrm{Ric}}
={}&\mathrm{Ric}-(n-2)B-(\operatorname{tr}_gB)g\\
={}&\mathrm{Ric}
-(n-2)(\nabla^2f-df\otimes df)
-\bigl(\Delta f+(n-2)|\nabla f|^2\bigr)g.
\end{aligned}
\]


Transformation of the Scalar Curvature
Taking the trace of \(\widetilde{\mathrm{Ric}}\) with respect to \(\widetilde{g}\), we find
\[
\widetilde{\mathrm{Sc}}
=\widetilde{g}^{ij}\widetilde{\mathrm{Ric}}_{ij}
=e^{-2f}g^{ij}\widetilde{\mathrm{Ric}}_{ij}.
\]
The \(g\)-trace of the Ricci transformation formula is
\[
g^{ij}\widetilde{\mathrm{Ric}}_{ij}
=\mathrm{Sc}-2(n-1)\Delta f
-(n-1)(n-2)|\nabla f|^2.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\widetilde{\mathrm{Sc}}
=e^{-2f}
\left[
\mathrm{Sc}-2(n-1)\Delta f
-(n-1)(n-2)|\nabla f|^2
\right].
\]


Transformation of the Unit Normal
Because \(\nu\) is a \(g\)-unit normal,
\[
g(\nu,\nu)=1.
\]
The vector field \(e^{-f}\nu\) remains normal to \(\Sigma\), and
\[
\widetilde{g}(e^{-f}\nu,e^{-f}\nu)
=e^{2f}g(e^{-f}\nu,e^{-f}\nu)=1.
\]
Hence
\[
\widetilde{\nu}=e^{-f}\nu.
\]


Transformation of the Second Fundamental Form
For \(X,Y\in T\Sigma\),
\[
\widetilde{A}(X,Y)
=\widetilde{g}(\widetilde{\nabla}_X\widetilde{\nu},Y).
\]
Using \(\widetilde{\nu}=e^{-f}\nu\), we compute
\[
\widetilde{\nabla}_X\widetilde{\nu}
=-e^{-f}X(f)\nu+e^{-f}\widetilde{\nabla}_X\nu.
\]
The connection transformation formula gives
\[
\widetilde{\nabla}_X\nu
=\nabla_X\nu+X(f)\nu+\nu(f)X,
\]
because \(g(X,\nu)=0\). Therefore,
\[
\widetilde{\nabla}_X\widetilde{\nu}
=e^{-f}\bigl(\nabla_X\nu+\nu(f)X\bigr).
\]
It follows that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\widetilde{A}(X,Y)
&=e^{2f}g\left(e^{-f}(\nabla_X\nu+\nu(f)X),Y\right)\\
&=e^f\bigl(A(X,Y)+\nu(f)g(X,Y)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]


Transformation of the Mean Curvature
Let \(\{e_1,\ldots,e_{n-1}\}\) be a local \(g\)-orthonormal frame tangent to \(\Sigma\). Then \(\{e^{-f}e_1,\ldots,e^{-f}e_{n-1}\}\) is \(\widetilde{g}\)-orthonormal. Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
\widetilde{H}
&=\sum_{a=1}^{n-1}\widetilde{A}(e^{-f}e_a,e^{-f}e_a)\\
&=e^{-f}\sum_{a=1}^{n-1}\bigl(A(e_a,e_a)+\nu(f)\bigr)\\
&=e^{-f}\bigl(H+(n-1)\nu(f)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus,
\[
\widetilde{H}
=e^{-f}\bigl(H+(n-1)\nu(f)\bigr).
\]
